text: The X-eye robotic system is precisely docked using a laser-guided crossbow for optical alignment. After placing the ports, the surgeon begins dissection below the inferior mesenteric artery using monopolar diathermy.
The X-eye system is precisely docked using a laser-guided crossbow for optics. Ports are placed, and dissection begins below the inferior mesenteric artery using monopolar diathermy.

The surgeon performs precise dissection at the back of the superior rectal artery, carefully isolating the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin. A window is created by dissecting around the artery, safeguarding the hypogastric nerve and left ureter. Hemolog clips are applied to secure the inferior mesenteric artery before it is divided using scissors.

The surgeon performs a precise dissection behind the superior rectal artery to avoid hypogastric nerve injury. After isolating the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, a window is created through careful dissection, protecting both the hypogastric nerve and the left ureter. Hemolog clips are applied to secure the inferior mesenteric artery before it is divided with scissors.

During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon divides the femoral ventricular vein close to the artery. Despite the patient's slim physique, the embryological plane in front of Gerota's fascia is visible, marked by a thin white line. Precise dissection is performed using a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors, employing paint-like movements to facilitate the procedure.

During the abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon divides the femoral ventricular vein close to the artery. Despite the patient's slim physique, the embryological plane in front of Gerota's fascia is visible, marked by a thin white line. Precise dissection is performed using paint-like movements with a monopolar diathermy hook or scissors.

The surgeon uses angulated robotic instruments to perform pelvic dissection, staying between the hypogastric nerves. The posterior dissection is followed by division of the peritoneum at the Douglas pouch, with careful protection of the pre-circle fissure, nerves, and the back wall of the vagina.

The surgeon uses angulated robotic instruments to perform pelvic dissection, staying between the hypogastric nerves. The posterior dissection is followed by division of the peritoneum at the Douglas pouch, ensuring protection of the pre-circle fissure, nerves, and the back wall of the vagina.

The surgeon performs precise dissection with monopolar diathermic scissors, applying gentle traction to facilitate posterior dissection. As the pelvic floor is approached, the specimen is mobilized from the left pelvic side wall. Anterior dissection is carried out close to the back wall of the vagina, where some tethering is observed.
The surgeon performs precise posterior dissection using monopolar diathermic scissors, applying gentle traction to separate tissue layers effectively.

The surgeon approaches the pelvic floor, focusing on mobilizing the specimen from the left pelvic side wall. Anterior dissection is performed close to the back wall of the vagina, where tethering is observed. The left pelvic side wall is carefully freed, ensuring minimal disruption to surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully frees the left pelvic side wall, ensuring the left ureter and hypogastric nerve are not inadvertently pulled with excessive friction on the specimen. The angulated instrument is used with precision in a high-definition view to navigate the tethering observed on the vaginal wall.

The left ureter and hypogastric left nerve are visible, with care taken to avoid pulling them with excessive friction on the specimen. The angulated instrument is used for precise dissection, shown in high definition. The tethered back wall of the vagina is dissected safely to avoid proximity to the tumor. The pelvic side wall on the left side reveals the pelvic floor.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tethered back wall of the vagina, progressing safely to avoid proximity to the tumor. The pelvic side wall on the left reveals the pelvic floor, with the back wall of the vagina visible anteriorly. A small piece of the backbone of the vagina is removed and blocked with the specimen to ensure an R-zero dissection, minimizing the risk of a positive margin.

The back wall of the vagina is visible anteriorly as the dissection progresses forward. A small piece of the vaginal backbone is removed along with the specimen to ensure an R-zero dissection, avoiding a positive margin. The dissection advances on both the right and left sides, revealing the endopelvic fascia and the pelvic floor.

The surgeon progresses the dissection forward on both the right and left sides, revealing the endopelvic fascia and pelvic floor. A gentle digital examination of the back wall of the vagina facilitates the identification of the outer lining layers, with a small disc of the vagina left intact from the specimen. As the dissection continues past this point, a clear, soft plane is visualized, allowing for further dissection in a completely bloodless field.

The surgeon performs a gentle digital examination on the back wall of the vagina, facilitating the dissection of the outer lining layers. A small disc of vaginal tissue is left intact from the specimen. As the dissection progresses, a clear soft plane is visualized, allowing for further dissection in a bloodless field. A swab is inserted into a small hole in the vagina to prevent gas leakage and maintain pneumoperitoneum.

A swab is inserted into a small hole in the vagina to minimize gas leak and maintain pneumoperitoneum. The camera is positioned at a thirty-degree upward angle, providing a static view of the pelvic floor muscles.

The camera is positioned at a thirty-degree upward angle to provide a static view of the pelvic floor muscles. The surgeon performs a modified abdominal perineal resection, carefully preserving most of the pelvic floor muscle while ensuring an R-zero dissection.

The surgeon performs a modified abdominoperineal resection, using angulated instruments and a stable camera view to precisely dissect the pelvic floor muscles. The dissection progresses safely and as low as possible, sacrificing minimal pelvic floor muscle to achieve R-zero resection.

The surgeon uses an angulated instrument and a stable camera view to perform precision surgery, progressing the dissection safely and deeply. The intersphincteric plane is entered posteriorly, confirmed by digital examination both luminally and externally on the skin.

The surgeon enters the intersphincteric plane posteriorly and confirms the location using digital examination both luminally and on the skin. The specimen is then mobilized further by cauterizing through to the skin extraluminally, completing the posterior dissection from above.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection by mobilizing the specimen onto the skin extraluminally, using cautery. The dissection progresses anteriorly with the left pelvic side wall visible, revealing the intersphincteric plane. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to maintain precision and anatomical clarity during the procedure.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection with gentle traction and counter traction, revealing the intersphincteric plane and pelvic side bowl. Using robotic assistance, circumferential dissection is carried out on both sides to reach the intersphincteric groove and skin edge. After complete specimen mobilization, a small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to perform circumferential dissection on both the left and right sides, entering the intersphincteric groove and reaching the skin edge. After complete mobilization of the specimen, a small defect in the vagina is closed with two interrupted POPDS stitches. The remaining task is to mobilize a small cuff of skin posteriorly from below to evacuate the specimen.

The surgeon mobilizes a small cuff of skin posteriorly from below to evacuate the specimen. The final step involves dividing the blood supply of the left colon proximally to create a left ventricular cyclostomy.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to divide the blood supply of the left colon proximally, preparing to fashion a left ventricular cyclostomy. The specimen is extracted from the perineum end, revealing a completely excised adenocarcinoma with clear margins upon examination.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to extract the specimen from the perineum. The excised specimen is shown, revealing a completely excised adenocarcinoma with clear margins.
